---
title : |
    Responses to Reviewers: 
    "Gender in Teaching: Insights from Five Million Syllabi on Collaboration, Interdisciplinarity, and Reading Selections"
subtitle: |
    Manuscript ID: PNASNEXUS-2025-00959-T
authors: 
    - Andrea Blasco
    - Elisa Orlandi

mainfont: Arial
fontsize: 12pt
linestretch: 1.5
geometry: "margin=0.5in"
bibliography: [../refs.bib]
--- 

Dear Editor and Reviewers, 

We thank you for the constructive and thoughtful feedback on our manuscript. We have revised the paper substantially to address all comments. Below, we respond point by point. Reviewer comments are in italics; our responses follow. Page and line references refer to the clean revised manuscript. 

# Editor Comments
 
**Thank you for considering PNAS Nexus as an outlet for your work. We have solicited and now received two in-depth reviews of your paper.**

**Both of the two reviewers found the overall quality of this paper was appreciable. Yet, in order to meet the criteria of publishing at PNAS Nexus, several critical revisions are needed. Given that the issues raised by the review team are rather serious, an ultimate acceptance cannot be guaranteed, though I do encourage you to submit a revised version to address all the questions raised by the reviewers. I summarize the core issues below.**

**First, the review team suggested you reorganized the introduction of the manuscript so as to further clarify the major focus of your research.**

We substantially revised the Introduction and literature review to sharpen the central research questions and highlight our main contributions. Following Reviewer 1's recommendation, the revised Introduction now contains clearly delineated subsections that address distinct strands of relevant literature: (i) how gender influences collaboration patterns, (ii) the representation of women in citations and course materials, (iii) the interdisciplinarity of mixed-gender teams, and (iv) the propensity of such teams to innovate. We believe this new structure contributes to clarify our contribution within the appropriate bodies of literature. We have also included a subsection about the literature on the impact of Covid-19 on academics, supporting our new analysis of pre-/post-pandemic team formation (see below).

**Second, the review team raised questions the different findings from prior research (e.g., Dion et al., 2018) and you may also need to address the issue regarding whether teachers can have freedom to choose whether to engage in team teaching, the incentives, and how all these may affect your findings.**

We added a paragraph comparing our findings to @dion2018gendered and related studies both in the Literature (Subsection 2.2 [p. 6]) and in the Discussion section (Discussion [p. 24]). We also expanded our discussion of institutional variation in team teaching (Discussion, [p. 26]). Specifically, we note that instructors' ability to engage in team teaching is shaped by administrative structures, credit allocation systems, and local policies that differ across institutions and national contexts. These factors may influence who opts into co-teaching and, consequently, the generalizability of our findings.

**Third, research methodology and interpretation of the results. Please follow the suggestions from the review team to address issues related to research methodology, including but not limited to use of controls, lack of representativeness of your data coverage, missing data, and measures (e.g., novelty). Please also pay attention to presentation and interpretation of your findings (e.g., temporal changes seen in the graphs). Would you be able to get more data and see the influence of COVID 19 on the findings? The review team also requested you run additional analyses (e.g., US versus non-US and by subject areas such as natural sciences, humanities, social sciences).**

We thank the review team for their detailed methodological suggestions. We have revised the manuscript accordingly and summarize the changes below:

* **Controls**: As requested by Reviewer 1, we added course-level indicators for basic, advanced, graduate and an "unknown" category, in addition to the existing controls in our regressions. The main results of our analysis remain the same.  

* **Representativeness**: We clarified that our dataset includes 50% of ROR education organizations in the selected countries, indicating a broad coverage. In response to Reviewer 1's comments, we conducted a detailed analysis of the representativeness of gender distribution (see discussion in Methods, Section 3.1.2 [p. 9] and the analysis is reported in the Supporting Information, Section SI-4). First, we examined the correlation between the women-to-men ratio in our data and the distribution of academic staff across countries. Second, we compared gender distributions among graduates by field of education in the US. Overall, our findings indicate a reasonable level of representativeness. However, because no comprehensive census of syllabi exists, institutional completeness cannot be fully verified; we explicitly acknowledge this limitation in the Discussion section.

* **Instructor gender identification**: We included a subsection in the Supporting Inoformation that describes in detail the gender disambiguiation process. We note that the process follows state-of-the-art methods (e.g., genderise.io), consistent with prior work, including Dion et al. [@dion2018gendered], and achieves  high accuracy. We also clarify that approximately 12% of instructors are classified as “unknown” due to the use of initials or abbreviated names (e.g., J. instead of John or Jane); See Section SI-1.1 [p. 48]. 

* **Novelty measures**: We expanded our measurement of course novelty to better capture its multidimensional nature. As suggested by Reviewer 1, we interpret the “age of readings” variable as a measure of readings' recency rather than novelty. In addition, we introduced two new measures: (1) a measure of the conventionality of readings, based on the number of citations a reading has received in syllabi published earlier; and (2) a measure of scientific novelty, calculated as the average Uzzi et al. [@uzzi2013atypical] atypicality score obtained from SciSciNet (v2) [@lin2023sciscinet]. Together, these three measures provide a more comprehensive assessment of course novelty.

* **Additional analyses**: We estimated separate models for U.S. vs. non-U.S. institutions and by disciplinary area (STEM vs. non-STEM). The main conclusions are consistent across these subsamples, though we observe variation in effect sizes between STEM and non-STEM fields. We present these results in a new Heterogeneity sub-section of the Results section [p. 20]. 

* **Temporal patterns**: We now model time trends (using a general addtive model) explicitly and present the estimated trajectories using smoothed curves in the figures. These models help reduce noise and highlight the underlying variation over time. Overall, we find that temporal patterns are modest and do not materially alter our main conclusions.

* **Presentation**: Reference groups are now standardized across all figures and specified explicitly in each caption. 

* **COVID-19**: As suggested by Reviewer 2, we expanded the dataset to include a new subset of 34 countries covering 2017–2022, enabling pre- and post-COVID analyses. We find that individual team formations declined more sharply than team-taught courses after COVID-19, reflecting broader contractions in course offerings. We also observe that mixed-gender teams were disproportionately affected during the post-COVID period, suggesting the pandemic may have disrupted formation of such teams. 

* **Robustness**: All robustness analyses are included in the Supplementary Information. 

\clearpage

# Responses to Reviewers

## Reviewer 1 

**The paper examines an interesting and important topic: how the composition of teaching teams in higher education matters for three pedagogical outcomes - the age of readings assigned, the gender of the authors of the readings assigned, and the interdisciplinarity of the course. This is an area lacking systematic quantitative analysis, and I believe that if the analysis and results become more improved and robust, it could make a contribution to the literature. However, a few problems exist that require revision.**

**The literature review needs to be re-organized into three subsections that clearly delineate the three arguments that the paper wants to make. * first, on "how courses taught by FEMALE instructors may contain more female authored readings".    * second, on "why MIXED-gender team would assign more novel readings".   * third, on "how MIXED-gender teams can increase interdisciplinary teaching"   * Currently, the structure looks very hidden to me, especially given that sometimes the authors discuss the effect of "female" while other times they discuss the effect of "mixed gender".**


We thank the reviewer for this helpful comment. We agree that the original literature review did not sufficiently highlight the conceptual foundation of our analysis. In response, we have restructured the literature into a new Literature section with clearly delineated subsections. Although the reviewer suggested a three-part organization, we separated gendered collaboration patterns from gendered course content to enhance conceptual clarity. The introductory paragraph to the revised literature review now reads:

> *Research shows that gender and institutional structures shape key aspects of academic work, from team formation to curriculum design. Four strands of this literature are particularly relevant to our study: (i) research on how gender influences collaboration patterns, (ii) the representation of women in citations and course materials, (iii) the interdisciplinarity of mixed-gender teams, and (iv) the propensity of such teams to innovate. These insights provide the conceptual foundation for our study, which we review in detail below*.

In addition, following Reviewer 2’s request to expand our analysis to the pre- and post-Covid-19 periods, we now include a fifth subsection summarizing the literature on how Covid-19 has affected academic collaborations. This addition further clarifies the context for our extended empirical analysis.

**There seems to be no control for the level of courses (ie., 101, 200+, 300+ and so on based on course code), but that could greatly influence the level of interdisciplinarity, age of course readings, or who teaches it. This is totally doable with Open Syllabus data to my knowledge**

We thank the reviewer for this valuable suggestion. While course level is not explicitly provided in the Open Syllabus data, it can be inferred from course codes (e.g., “Psychology 101” typically indicating an introductory course, and “Psychology 401” an advanced one). Following @biasi2022education, we classified courses into four levels based on their codes (basic, advanced, graduate or unknown); their validation suggests this approach achieves roughly 85% accuracy in the U.S. context. Although no equivalent validation exists for European institutions, and accuracy may therefore be lower, we additionally used the number of assigned readings in each syllabus as a further proxy for course level. Our results remain robust when incorporating these measures. 

In the revised manuscript, we discuss the issue in the Methods section [p. 9], all regressions results reported in the Results section (Figure 7 to 12) include course level controls, and we detailed the process of code-level extraction in the Supporting Information, Section SI-1.2 [p. 48].

**Although it is understandable to not have perfectly representative coverage of fields and schools given the scraped nature of this online based dataset -- this lack of representativeness is indeed heavily related to the aggregated estimates that the authors presented in the graphs throughout the results section. For example, when estimating whether mixed gender teaching groups have more interdisciplinarity than male-only groups, we need to know whether both types of teaching groups that are used in the analysis are proportional to the real distribution of the specific discipline in the specific institution and the specific country. Otherwise, how can we be sure that the aggregated / average estimates are not affected by the distribution of the cases selected in the operational data in this study?** 

We thank the reviewer for raising this important concern about representativeness and its implications for the aggregated estimates presented in the results. We agree that understanding how well our dataset reflects the underlying disciplinary, institutional, and national distributions is essential. In response, we have expanded both the Methods and Supporting Information to make the representativeness assessment more explicit and transparent. 

Specifically, we compared the proportions of women and men instructors in our dataset with official statistics on academic staff across countries (Section SI-4, [p. 51]). This analysis shows strong correlations for most countries, particularly English-speaking ones, where coverage of syllabi is highest. We also examined representativeness at the field level by correlating the gender distribution in our data with U.S. NCSES statistics on Ph.D. graduates by academic field (Section SI-4, [p. 51]). Here too we observe high correlations in many major fields. Taken together, these analyses indicate that the dataset is reasonably representative for the purposes of our study. 

Yet, despite our efforts there is no single source available to us allowing a test for the representativeness of gender distribution in course syllabi. We discuss this limitation in the Discussion section. 
<!-- Yet, we note that our sample covers 50% of the educational organisations listed in the Research Organisation Regstry, indicating a broad coverage.  -->

**Genderizer: 12% of the syllabi were reported to have unknown gender (p 5). Is this a problem of missing, or is it a problem of the genderizer being unable to estimate the gender (many asian names are gender neutral) -- how is this going to affect the estimates?**

We thank the reviewer for the opportunity to clarify this point. In the Supporting Information, we have included a section on gender that describes the process of gender disambiguation (Section SI-1.1, [p. 48]). In brief, OpenSyllabus uses a version of Genderize.io, which has an estimated overall accuracy of 98% in academic publications. If the probability of an author being female exceeds 0.5, the author is classified as female; otherwise, they are classified as male. However, not all instructors listed in the syllabi provide their full first names; some list only initials (e.g., "J." for "John" or "Jane"). Only in such cases, OpenSyllabus assigns the author to the "unknown" category.

**novelty = using more recent readings in the syllabus: a decision the authors tried to defend on page 6. However, I do not really buy it. It is NOT a conventional construction of novelty simply by using the age of readings (especially given that the authors are aware that STS literature developed much better measurements of it) -- for example, old readings can also be rediscovered as highly novel in new contexts where they regain relevance and attention that they did not have before.** 

Thank you for this insightful comment. We agree with your point and have revised the manuscript accordingly.
 
First, we now clarify that measuring novelty in the context of syllabi is inherently challenging. Unlike research articles, which draw on rich and diverse bibliographic networks, syllabi typically cite only a small number of textbooks. This limited citation base makes it difficult to apply more conventional novelty metrics, which rely on detecting atypical or unconventional combinations of sources. Moreover, novelty measured at the time of publication may not reflect the novelty students experience at the time of teaching; i.e., a paper that was highly novel in year x may be perceived as well-established or even dated when incorporated into a later syllabus. We discuss these challenges in the Methods section (sub-sec. 3.2.2 [p. 11]) and Limitations sub-section [pp. 24-25]. 
 
Second, your comment helped us recognise that teaching novelty is multidimensional. An instructor may rely on older material but use it in pedagogically innovative ways (“teaching innovation”). Alternatively, they may assign papers at the frontier of scientific knowledge (“scientific novelty”). Or they may introduce newer readings (“recency”).In response, we have revised our measurement strategy to address these distinctions (see Methods, Subsection 3.1.2 [p. 12]):
 
1. We no longer label the age of readings as “novelty.” Instead, we now explicitly refer to this dimension as the recency of assigned materials.
2. We introduce two new novelty measures to more appropriately capture the multidimensional nature of teaching innovation:
    - Teaching conventionality: the number of times the same resource appears in other syllabi. Often used readings indicate more conventional teaching.
    - Scientific novelty: the Uzzi et al [@uzzi2013atypical]'s atypicality of readings at the time they were taught, which we compute by matching the readings DOIs to SciSciNet (v2), a large dataset of pre-computed measures of novelty [@lin2023sciscinet].
 
These changes improve conceptual clarity and allow us to more accurately capture different forms of novelty relevant to teaching. 

**On page 10, I found myself wondering why the figures tend to have different reference groups every now and then. For example, in section 3.1.3, the authors wrote, "between 2000 and 2019 courses taught by a single female instructor ... ...than otherwise similar courses taught by **a single man** in that year... ... Similarly, courses taught by two women also assign newer materials than those taught by **two men** " -- this seems to me that the reference group in the first comparison is "courses taught by single man" while the reference group for the second is "courses taught by two men".  This is highly confusing because in the note of the specific figure, it says the reference is "courses taught by single male" . If different reference groups are used, then it must be clarified early, and in the figures' notes as well**

Thank you for the comment. We standardized reference categories across all models and clearly report them in figure captions (e.g., “single male instructor” as the baseline). 

\clearpage

## Reviewer 2

**In this study, the authors analyze 2.9 million syllabi across 15 countries from 1990 to 2019 to assess how the instructors’ sex (individually or in teams) influences the degree of interdisciplinarity, the average age of assigned readings, and the percentage of female authors included in the syllabus. A linear mixed effects model shows that female teams and mixed gender teams assign more work by women scholars and that the interdisciplinarity range is higher in these instances. Female instructors/teams tend to assign newer work as well. The study leverages an interesting dataset and uses a clever research design including 1) text analysis (bag of words) to capture a syllabus’ level of interdisciplinarity and 2) a Monte Carlo randomization approach to assess actual gender distributions of teaching teams and what is expected based on institutional data. The study also provides insights into how team-teaching influences syllabus content, especially in terms of the sex of the teams (MM, MF, FF). The findings will be of interest to readers of the journal.**

**The finding that female teaching teams tend to include more readings by female authors accords with findings from the citation literature. See for example: Dion, Michelle L., Jane Lawrence Sumner, and Sara McLaughlin Mitchell. 2018. “Gendered citation patterns across political science and social science methodology fields.” Political Analysis 26(3): 312-327. On the other hand, Dion et al do not find that mixed gender teams cite more work by women in research articles than male teams, so there is an interesting bit of difference in these results.**

Thank you for the reference. We have now highlighted it into the literature review and discussed its relevance in the Discussion section: 

> *These findings align with prior research on gendered citation patterns in the academic literature [e.g., @dion2018gendered], which shows that female scholars are more likely to cite work by other women. Interestingly, Dion et al. find that mixed-gender research teams do not cite more women than male-only teams, highlighting a divergence from our course-level results. Taken together, our analysis suggests that instructor gender shapes reading lists in ways that may differ from research citation patterns and that mixed-gender teaching teams could foster greater gender diversity in assigned readings.*

**There are a lot of temporal changes seen in the graphs and I'm wondering what is driving them. In many cases, the expansion towards more mixed/female team teaching seems to experience a backsliding in the last few years of the data. I think the authors should try to identify what may be driving these changes. It’s unfortunate that the data end in 2019 because it would be interesting to see how the Covid-19 pandemic altered these patterns further.**

We have substantially expanded our analysis of temporal patterns in response to the reviewer’s comment.

**Tema formation:** We find that the persistent increase in the proportion of single-gender (women-only) team formation is consistent with the growing representation of women in the academic workforce over 2000–2019. In contrast, the relative stability of mixed-gender team formation suggests persistent structural or social barriers that limit the growth of mixed collaborations, even as the overall gender composition of faculty changes.

**Associations between team composition and outcomes** While there are some year-to-year fluctuations in the associations between team composition and the selected outcomes, these differences are rarely statistically significant and appear largely attributable to noise. However, to better highlight systematic temporal patterns, we have modeled these time trends explicitly, averaging the estimated effects over time using a generalized additive model, weighting years according to estimation accuracy (i.e., 1/std. error^2). The results are presented in all figures as smoothed curves that overly the year-specific estimates. After applying this approach, we observe that the association between mixed-gender teams and the age of readings decreases over time. One possible explanation is the evolving demographic composition of women in academia, who—on average—may be more senior in later periods and therefore assign less recent readings. We now discuss this possibility in the revised Discussion section [p. 23].

**There’s almost an assumption that faculty can engage in team teaching if they desire, but that is not true in many disciplines or institutions. This possibility is briefly mentioned on page 11, but I think you should discuss this more. In my institution, it would be difficult to get credit for team teaching. I teach 4 courses per year and typically any team teaching would be done as an overload beyond the 4 courses. I realize your fixed effects for institutions and countries help to pick that up, but I'm curious what the data looks like descriptively across countries.**

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point and share the same concern. Unfortunately, we cannot test this empirically with our current data. Instead, we have added a discussion of this issue in the Limitations sub-section [p. 25], which reads:

> *Fourth, institutional policies regarding teaching loads and the recognition of co-teaching
vary substantially across countries and institutions. In many universities, team teaching
does not fully count toward teaching loads or is treated as an overload, limiting faculty op-
portunities to engage in it even if they wish to. Moreover, teaching teams are not always de-
termined by individual instructors’ preferences: in many institutions, course assignments
and team composition are partially or entirely determined at the departmental or program
level—for example, by course coordinators, curriculum structures, or administrative rules.
In such cases, team formation may reflect institutional or organizational preferences rather
than instructors’ choices. Our data do not capture these institutional constraints or assign-
ment mechanisms, which may mechanically reduce or shape the observed incidence of
mixed-gender teams. Consequently, our descriptive patterns reflect both individual prefer-
ences and the structural opportunities and assignment practices provided by institutions.*

**Similarly, the data is very U.S. dominant (66% of all syllabi). What if you run a model for U.S. institutions and compare that to a model with institutions in non-U.S. countries? Are the results similar or different? Why is team teaching less common in EU countries? This is the kind of thing I wanted more discussion of and some analyses to explore these differences.**

We appreciate this comment. As noted, we already discuss cross-country differences in team formation. Following your suggestion, we expanded this analysis to examine potential heterogeneity in the association between team composition and course readings across countries. Specifically, we compared the United States to all non-U.S. institutions with respect to (1) novelty, (2) female-authored readings, and (3) interdisciplinarity. The results reveal no systematic deviations across these outcomes: the estimates for U.S. institutions closely track those obtained for the full sample, suggesting that the main patterns we document are robust across national contexts. We now report these findings in the Supporting Information (Figure SI-4, p. 51).

**It could be interesting to run separate analyses in general areas as well (e.g., sciences, humanities, social sciences) to see if the general patterns generalize across these areas.**

Many thanks for this helpful suggestion. In response, we conducted additional heterogeneity analysis across major disciplinary areas. Specifically, we compared STEM against non-STEM fields. This exercise revealed several noteworthy differences:  while the direction of the associations is consistent across fields, the magnitude of the effects varies. We now report these results in a new subsection on Heterogeneity (pp. 19-20). The new paragraph on STEM fields reads:

> *Figure 12 highlights several key differences between STEM and non-STEM fields, based on separate models estimated for STEM fields compared against results from the full sample. The gap in female-author citations associated with women instructors remains consistent but is significantly smaller in STEM fields, roughly half the size observed in all fields. The positive association between mixed-gender teams and conventional readings is larger in STEM fields. Conversely, the gap in atypicality widens for women teaching alone, although the effect has been diminishing over time. In contrast, the gap in the age of assigned readings between women and men teaching alone is smaller in STEM. With respect to interdisciplinarity, the estimated differences are small and not consistently significant across years. Together, these results provide strong evidence of heterogeneity between STEM and non-STEM fields in the strength of the associations between team composition and course materials.*

# References
